---
title: "Tutorial 1: Getting Started with Open Science Pillars"
subtitle: "Install the core plugin, orient, and run your first honest analysis (~10 minutes)"
format: html
---

<!-- Surfaces verified: Claude Code 2026-07-05 (fresh install, timed: 4.6 minutes end to end).
     Cowork and Claude Science: pending; conversational phrasings below
     are the surface-parity prompts from marketplace/docs/prompts/. -->

**Surfaces verified:** Claude Code (2026-07-05). Cowork and Claude
Science pending; every step below works conversationally, and the exact
phrasings are the ones the project tests with.

## What you will do

Install the core plugin, let it orient you, generate a small test
dataset, quality-control it, and produce an anomaly analysis with a
map, with the plugin's guardrails (area weighting, stated baselines,
QC before statistics) doing their job around you.

## 1. Install (2 minutes)

On Claude Code:

```bash
claude plugin marketplace add open-science-pillars/marketplace
claude plugin install core@open-science-pillars
```

On Cowork or Claude Science: add the marketplace
`open-science-pillars/marketplace` in your plugin settings and install
`core` from it.

Python side (once): the analyses run in your Python environment. For this
tutorial you need `xarray`, `netcdf4`, `matplotlib`, and `pymannkendall`:

```bash
pip install xarray netcdf4 matplotlib pymannkendall
```

(Ocean tutorials add a few more packages, listed where they're used.) The
[glossary](https://github.com/open-science-pillars/marketplace/blob/main/GLOSSARY.md#prerequisites-at-a-glance)
has the full prerequisites at a glance.

## 2. Orient (1 minute)

Ask, in your own words or exactly this:

> What science tools do I have set up here, and what should I do next?

You get one screen: installed plugins, connector status, local config,
the workflow skills, and one suggested next step. On Claude Code,
`/core:start` does the same.

## 3. Get data to play with (2 minutes)

Core ships a reproducible synthetic climate dataset for exactly this
purpose (real-data workflows come in Tutorial 2). Ask:

> Generate the core plugin's test fixture and tell me what's in it.

Claude runs the plugin's `verification/fixtures/make_fixtures.py` and
summarizes: 40 years of monthly global 2 m temperature on a 5 degree
grid, with a constructed warming trend. The summary you get back IS the
first lesson: dimensions, coordinate conventions, calendar, units, and
fill-value status, checked before anything else.

## 4. Quality control (2 minutes)

> Run quality control on the fixture file.

Watch for the six checks (coordinates/time, fill values, completeness,
physical bounds, QA flags, discontinuities), each with a verdict and
evidence. Try the bad-data variant too if you are curious
(`era5like_t2m_bad.nc`, generated alongside): three defects are hiding
in it, and the QC pass finds all three without being told.

## 5. Anomalies and a map (3 minutes)

> Compute annual global mean temperature anomalies against a 1991-2020
> baseline and show me a trend map with significance marked.

Two guardrails fire silently here, and one loudly:

- the spatial mean is area-weighted (unweighted means on a lat-lon grid
  are wrong by several kelvin on this fixture, by construction);
- the baseline is stated with the result, every time;
- the trend map's significance marking is explained in its caption
  (test and level), per the figure rules.

## 6. Where to go next

- **Tutorial 2** does this against real NASA ocean data: ECCO heat
  transport with observational comparison, and a SWOT scene.
- Ask `what data should I use for <your question>?` to see gated
  discovery with the knowledge bundle's caveats attached.
- Everything you saw is inspectable: the skills, the knowledge concepts
  with their evidence links, and the verification notebooks live in the
  [core repository](https://github.com/open-science-pillars/core).

**Timing note:** the walkthrough above was executed fresh-install to
fresh-output in 4.6 minutes on 2026-07-05 (measured; your Python
environment resolve time may vary).
